FT_IC1.evidence: N=9,066 children from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study at baseline (ages 9-10) and 1-year follow-up; ABCD data release 4.0 used; 21 recruitment sites confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal cumulative logistic regression (VGAM package) modeling psychiatric symptoms as predictors of screen time one year later, and screen time as predictors of psychiatric symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2024) 59:2279-2290.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social screen time composite is defined in methods as summing time spent texting, using social media, and video chatting — all phone/SM-specific items. Numeric ORs are reported for social screen time as DV paired with individual-trait psychiatric symptoms (e.g., clinical externalizing predicting weekday social screen time OR=1.15, 95% CI 1.14-1.15; internalizing predicting weekday social screen time OR=1.17, 95% CI 1.17-1.18). Psychiatric symptoms (internalizing, externalizing, attention via CBCL) are individual-trait-level outcomes on the other side.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numerous OR estimates with 95% CIs reported throughout for psychiatric symptoms predicting social and nonsocial screen time, and for screen time predicting psychiatric symptoms.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as FT_IC3: quantitative results (ORs) reported for all key associations.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone (social screen time) appears as DV in the primary analyses (psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time) but also as IV in the supplementary analyses (screen time predicting future psychiatric symptoms, e.g., weekday social screen time predicting clinical total psychiatric problems OR=1.11, 95% CI 1.10-1.12). Because SM appears as IV in at least one analysis, EC7 does not apply.
notes: Bidirectional design with SM as both IV and DV across primary and supplementary analyses. Social screen time composite (texting + social media + video chatting) passes Step 1 directly. Nonsocial screen time (YouTube, TV, video games) would not qualify alone, but qualifying social screen time estimates exist throughout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study explicitly uses the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) dataset; approximately 12,000 participants aged 9-13; baseline n=7,107 and 2-year follow-up n=4,505; longitudinal multisite U.S. NIH-funded study confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models (LME), one-sample Mendelian randomization, two-sample MR, and mediation/two-step MR analysis applied to ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Advanced Science (2024), DOI 10.1002/advs.202307540.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SNS (visiting social networking sites) and Text (texting on a cell phone, tablet, or computer) and VC (video chatting) are phone/SM-specific variables each with their own beta coefficients. SNS use is significantly associated with picture vocabulary (beta=-4.28 and -1.31 at two time points, p<10^-4) and picture sequence memory (beta=-3.36 and -1.33, p<10^-6) and rule-breaking behavior (beta=0.73 and 0.42, p<10^-4) — all individual-trait-level outcomes. VC use is negatively associated with picture vocabulary (beta=-2.11 and -2.64, p<10^-6) and picture sequence memory (beta=-1.46 and -1.90, p<0.001).

FT_IC3.evidence: Beta coefficients from LME models and Mendelian randomization effect estimates reported throughout Table S1, Figure 2, Figure 3, and Table 2 for screen use variables (including SNS, Text, VC) paired with cognitive and behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as FT_IC3: quantitative beta coefficients, 95% CIs, p-values, and MR causal estimates reported.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen use variables (including SNS, VC, Text) serve as IV in primary LME and forward MR analyses predicting cognitive and behavioral outcomes. Additionally, reverse MR analysis has screen use as DV (cognitive/behavioral traits predicting screen use). Bidirectional design; SM/phone appears as IV in multiple analyses.
notes: The six screen use types include TV/movies (TVM), Video, Game, Text, SNS, and VC. TVM and Game would not qualify alone, but SNS (social networking), Text (phone texting), and VC (video chatting) are phone/SM-specific with their own coefficients paired with individual-trait outcomes (cognitive scores, CBCL behavioral subscales). Step 1 is clearly met.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, years 2-4 visits, n=6,143 adolescents, 21 U.S. research sites, confirmed ABCD Study 5.1 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Bayesian hierarchical linear models examining longitudinal association between fMRI-derived reward processing (MID task ROI beta-weights) and VGAQ scores over years 2-4.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions 13(4):1051-1063 (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: The only screen/media variable is the VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), which measures generic video game addiction symptoms. Per binding clarification: VGAQ is a gaming-addiction scale without explicit mobile/phone modality specification; the paper states participants played 'single-player or online multiplayer video games' with no restriction to phone-based gaming. No separate phone/SM variable is measured or analyzed anywhere in the paper. Gaming alone (VGAQ, unspecified platform) fails IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based or paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable. Neither condition is met.

notes: Paper examines neural reward processing (caudate activation during MID task) predicting VGAQ scores over time. VGAQ is a video game addiction questionnaire with no explicit mobile/phone modality. Excluded under FT-EC2.
